description: Explains how marimo notebooks are run as web apps, ordinary Python scripts, exported artifacts, and browser-only WebAssembly notebooks.
document:
marimo treats a notebook as a reusable Python program that can be presented in several ways depending on how it is launched. The app path is the interactive, server-backed presentation: `marimo run` starts a web server, hides code by default, and lays out notebook outputs as an application. A single notebook becomes a direct app, while multiple notebooks or a directory become a gallery whose cards can draw title, description, and thumbnail metadata from the notebooks. App layout is part of the notebook experience rather than a separate framework. The default vertical layout concatenates outputs, the grid editor stores drag-and-drop placement in a layouts folder that should be shared with the notebook, and the slides layout presents outputs in notebook order with code hidden.

The server startup path in `marimo/_server/start.py` turns those app choices into runtime state. It chooses a port when none is supplied, reads configuration from the selected notebook or directory, and constructs a session manager with the file router, run or edit mode, code visibility, authentication token, command-line arguments, watch settings, sandbox mode, and optional app isolation for multi-app servers. Folder galleries in watch mode are discovered dynamically, and the implementation warns that newly added notebooks may appear and execute when opened, so trusted directories and auth controls matter. The server also resolves proxy-facing host and port information, initializes the Starlette app, applies shared state, and runs uvicorn with settings tuned differently for edit and run mode.

Running a notebook as a script is deliberately simpler. The guide describes marimo notebooks as Python files that can be invoked with the Python interpreter, useful for side effects such as writing files and for console output in a terminal. Script execution uses normal Python command-line argument mechanisms such as sys.argv, so notebook code can use familiar parsers and branch between notebook defaults and script-provided values. The same file can expose reusable functions and classes as an importable module. When users want a flat script artifact instead of executing the notebook file directly, the export guide points to a topologically ordered Python export, with the important caveat that top-level await is not supported by that flat script format even though direct notebook execution still works.

marimo's export layer separates static snapshots from browser-executable notebooks. Static HTML export runs the notebook, serializes the notebook and session view, optionally strips code and console output, and inlines supported virtual files as data URIs with a size limit so exported pages do not silently contain broken media. WebAssembly export is handled by `Exporter.export_as_wasm`, which builds a WASM notebook template from the notebook code, app configuration, display configuration, selected edit or run mode, code visibility, and optional asset URL. The CLI command for HTML WASM copies marimo's static assets into the output directory, creates a marker for GitHub Pages compatibility, copies a neighboring public data folder when present, and reminds users that the result must be served over HTTP rather than opened through a file URL.

WASM notebooks move Python execution into the browser through Pyodide. The docs position them as a good fit for sharing, education, lightweight exploration, and small tools because users do not need local Python or a hosted Python backend. Packages are constrained by what Pyodide can load: many scientific packages are preinstalled, pure Python wheels can work, missing imports may be installed automatically, and explicit installation can use micropip. Data for exported WASM notebooks can be placed in a public folder beside the notebook and accessed through the notebook location helper so the same path works locally and after export.

The Pyodide implementation mirrors a normal marimo session but replaces the server connection with browser-callable bridge methods. `create_session` builds an app file manager, merges default and inline script configuration, sends an early kernel-ready message to the frontend, and returns a session plus bridge. The session owns asynchronous queues for control messages, UI/model updates, completions, and user input. Its kernel task patches Pyodide-specific networking and recursion behavior, creates streams that send cell notifications back to the frontend, and listens for kernel commands and completions. The bridge exposes operations the browser needs, including reading and saving code, formatting cells, file management, completion, input delivery, and exporting the current session to HTML.
